topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee mug slipped from my numb fingers, shattering on the hardwood floor with a sound that seemed impossibly loud in the quiet apartment. I stared at Liam, his face open and earnest as he spoke about a shared memory—a ridiculous summer adventure we’d had at ten. "Remember that time we tried to build a raft out of old tires?" he asked, a wide, easy smile lighting up his features. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the motion painful. I shook my head slowly, a jerky, involuntary movement. "No," I managed, the word sounding thin and brittle. He blinked, the smile faltering, replaced by a flicker of confusion that quickly morphed into something softer, something distant. He looked at me, truly looked, and then he just shrugged, as if the question itself had been misplaced. A cold, heavy sensation settled in my chest, pressing down on my lungs. I wanted to bolt, to disappear into the wallpaper, but my feet were rooted to the spot. The air felt thick, suffocating.

[story 2]

I watched him talk, my gaze locked onto the familiar curve of his jaw, the way his eyes crinkled at the corners when he laughed. Every anecdote he shared was a tapestry woven with threads I thought were ours—secret codes, inside jokes, shared traumas. But when he reached for the specific detail I’d been anticipating, the connection snapped. His brow furrowed, a subtle tightening around his eyes, and then he just… looked through me. It was like watching a perfectly rendered movie where I was the only actor who hadn't been cast. My hands started to tremble, the tremor running up my arms until my fingers felt clumsy and unresponsive. I forced a breath in, trying to keep my voice steady, but it came out shaky, high-pitched. "That's not right," I whispered, the sound barely audible over the hum of the refrigerator. He offered a sympathetic nod, his expression devoid of any recognition, a polite acknowledgment of a stranger. The silence that followed was vast, echoing the sudden, terrifying emptiness where our history used to reside.

[story 3]

It happened over lukewarm pizza and the low glow of the television. Liam was recounting a story about a disastrous school play, his voice animated, full of genuine recollection. I leaned forward, eager to interject with the shared context, the way we always did. "Wait, that was when Sarah tripped over the curtain—" I started, my voice gaining momentum, feeling a rush of familiarity. He stopped mid-sentence, turning his head slowly toward me. His gaze swept over my face, lingering for a fraction of a second, but it didn't settle. It was the blank appraisal one gives a passing acquaintance. He frowned slightly, a small crease forming between his eyebrows, then he redirected his attention to the screen, dismissing me entirely. A strange, dizzying sensation washed over me, like the floor tilting beneath me. My mouth went dry, and I felt a sudden, desperate urge to physically recoil. I couldn't speak; the words caught in my throat, leaving me stranded in a sudden, profound isolation, watching my oldest friend exist in a reality where I was simply absent.
